title: Joint Aspiration
oneLiner: Bedside procedure to aspirate synovial fluid from an acutely swollen joint; essential to diagnose septic arthritis or crystal arthropathy in monoarthritis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic arthritis (untreated):** joint destruction begins within 8–24 hours; can progress to septic shock. **Single finding:** a red, hot, swollen, painful single joint *with fever* — start empiric IV antibiotics within 1 hour even if aspiration is delayed.
- **Necrotising fasciitis (rare, limb-threatening):** pain out of proportion to exam, systemic toxicity, rapid spread. **Single finding:** crepitus or bullae + shock — do **not** aspirate; escalate immediately for surgical debridement.
- **Compartment syndrome (post-traumatic or post-procedure):** tense swelling, pain on passive stretch, paraesthesia. **Single finding:** severe pain with passive toe/finger flexion — urgent fasciotomy needed.
sections.Presentation:
- Acute monoarthritis: red, hot, swollen joint with severe pain and restricted range of movement
- Commonest joints: knee > hip > shoulder > ankle > wrist
- May have fever, rigors, inability to weight-bear
- Ask about: recent joint surgery, penetrating trauma, skin infection, IVDU, prosthetic joint, immunosuppression (steroids, biologics, HIV)
- Bloody aspirate + trauma/anticoagulation → haemarthrosis
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - Septic arthritis (fever, severe pain, single joint; Gram stain + culture positive)
  - Gout (sudden onset >24h, hyperuricaemia, negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals)
  - Pseudogout (older; knee/wrist, positively birefringent rhomboids, chondrocalcinosis on XR)
  - Reactive arthritis (recent GI/GU infection, asymmetrical lower limb, tendinitis)
  - Haemarthrosis (trauma/anticoagulation, bloody fluid, no crystals)
  - Flare of inflammatory arthritis (polyarticular, symmetrical, known rheumatoid/psoriatic)
- **Splitter:** **Gram stain + polarised microscopy** — Gram-positive cocci = septic; negatively birefringent crystals = gout; positively birefringent = pseudogout.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Sterile aspiration with 1% lidocaine (no adrenaline) — see technique below  
  - Note fluid: cloudy/purulent → septic; clear/yellow → non-inflammatory; bloody → trauma/coagulopathy; milky → pseudogout  
  - Send fluid for: Gram stain + culture (+ extended culture if anaerobes suspected), crystal analysis (polarised light), cell count + differential, glucose/protein (optional)
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP, ESR  
  - Blood cultures (2 sets)  
  - Urate (note: can be normal in acute gout flare)  
  - Renal function, clotting screen, INR (if on warfarin)
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: X-ray of joint (fracture, osteomyelitis, chondrocalcinosis, OA)  
  - Second-line: Ultrasound (confirms effusion, guides aspiration — essential for hip)  
  - MRI: if suspect osteomyelitis or deep infection not accessible by aspiration (e.g., hip)
- **Special tests:**  
  - Ultrasound guidance for hip aspiration (especially in children; do not attempt blind)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - Kocher criteria for septic arthritis in children (fever, non-weightbearing, ESR>40, WBC>12) — not absolute; clinical suspicion overrides  
  - In adults: treat as septic until proven otherwise if fever + monoarthritis + high CRP
- **Never-miss test:** **Synovial fluid culture + Gram stain** before antibiotics — omission delays diagnosis and destroys joint.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Assess for sepsis: NEWS2, lactate, blood cultures  
  - If septic arthritis suspected: **start empiric IV antibiotics after cultures, but do not delay >1 hour** (even if aspiration fails)  
  - Aspirate joint aseptically (see Drugs & Doses for technique)  
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g PO/IV; avoid NSAIDs until infection excluded
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue IV antibiotics (narrow once sensitivity back)  
  - Daily joint assessment (pain, range, redness, temperature)  
  - Orthopaedic referral for **surgical washout** if:  
    - Hip joint (difficult to drain)  
    - Prosthetic joint  
    - No improvement after 48h medical management  
    - Hypotension/shock or compartment syndrome
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Septic arthritis: IV antibiotics ≥2 weeks then oral switch; total ≥6 weeks (native), longer for prosthetic  
  - Crystal arthropathy: NSAIDs or colchicine for acute flare; urate-lowering therapy (allopurinol) only after flare settled
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline if septic (rate per sepsis protocol)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if immobilised/high risk (but withhold if haemarthrosis)  
  - Diet: normal  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (more if septic)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Empiric IV antibiotics for septic arthritis (NICE):**  
  - Flucloxacillin 2g IV QDS (native joint)  
  - If penicillin allergy: clindamycin 600mg IV QDS or teicoplanin [check local policy]  
  - If MRSA suspected: add vancomycin (loading dose 15–20 mg/kg IV, then monitor trough)  
  - Prosthetic joint: discuss with microbiology — often flucloxacillin + rifampicin or alternative
- **Local anaesthetic for aspiration:**  
  - Lidocaine 1% (10 mg/mL) without adrenaline  
  - Max dose: 3 mg/kg (e.g., ~21 mL for 70 kg) — **do not exceed**  
  - Typical volume for knee: 5–10 mL. Use 25G needle for skin wheal, then 21G for deep.
- **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g PO/IV QDS. If infection excluded, NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen 500mg PO BD) — contraindicated in AKI, asthma, GI bleeding.
- **Colchicine for acute gout:** 0.5mg PO TDS for 2–3 days. **Reduce in renal impairment** (eGFR <30 → not recommended)
- **Key interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs + anticoagulants → ↑ bleeding risk  
  - Colchicine + macrolides (e.g., clarithromycin) → fatal toxicity — **check all current drugs**
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Vancomycin: dose based on renal function and trough levels  
  - Colchicine: avoid if eGFR <10
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Vancomycin — failure to give loading dose AND check trough after 24h** → toxicity (nephrotoxicity/ototoxicity)  
  - **Colchicine — overdose (max 6mg total course).** Fatal if >0.5mg TDS in elderly / renal impairment. Do not co-prescribe with P-gp or CYP3A4 inhibitors.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Iatrogenic septic arthritis (sterile technique crucial)  
  - Haemarthrosis (especially if INR >3 or platelets <50)  
  - Cartilage damage from needle, nerve/vascular injury (rare: popliteal artery, tibial nerve)  
  - Vasovagal syncope  
  - Cellulitis at puncture site
- **Chronic:**  
  - Joint destruction (septic arthritis — cartilage loss within 8–24h)  
  - Chronic pain, stiffness, secondary OA  
  - Relapse of infection (especially prosthetic joints)  
  - Antibiotic complications: C

rawMarkdown:
## Joint Aspiration
**One-liner:** Bedside procedure to aspirate synovial fluid from an acutely swollen joint; essential to diagnose septic arthritis or crystal arthropathy in monoarthritis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic arthritis (untreated):** joint destruction begins within 8–24 hours; can progress to septic shock. **Single finding:** a red, hot, swollen, painful single joint *with fever* — start empiric IV antibiotics within 1 hour even if aspiration is delayed.
- **Necrotising fasciitis (rare, limb-threatening):** pain out of proportion to exam, systemic toxicity, rapid spread. **Single finding:** crepitus or bullae + shock — do **not** aspirate; escalate immediately for surgical debridement.
- **Compartment syndrome (post-traumatic or post-procedure):** tense swelling, pain on passive stretch, paraesthesia. **Single finding:** severe pain with passive toe/finger flexion — urgent fasciotomy needed.

### Presentation
- Acute monoarthritis: red, hot, swollen joint with severe pain and restricted range of movement
- Commonest joints: knee > hip > shoulder > ankle > wrist
- May have fever, rigors, inability to weight-bear
- Ask about: recent joint surgery, penetrating trauma, skin infection, IVDU, prosthetic joint, immunosuppression (steroids, biologics, HIV)
- Bloody aspirate + trauma/anticoagulation → haemarthrosis

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - Septic arthritis (fever, severe pain, single joint; Gram stain + culture positive)
  - Gout (sudden onset >24h, hyperuricaemia, negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals)
  - Pseudogout (older; knee/wrist, positively birefringent rhomboids, chondrocalcinosis on XR)
  - Reactive arthritis (recent GI/GU infection, asymmetrical lower limb, tendinitis)
  - Haemarthrosis (trauma/anticoagulation, bloody fluid, no crystals)
  - Flare of inflammatory arthritis (polyarticular, symmetrical, known rheumatoid/psoriatic)
- **Splitter:** **Gram stain + polarised microscopy** — Gram-positive cocci = septic; negatively birefringent crystals = gout; positively birefringent = pseudogout.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Sterile aspiration with 1% lidocaine (no adrenaline) — see technique below  
  - Note fluid: cloudy/purulent → septic; clear/yellow → non-inflammatory; bloody → trauma/coagulopathy; milky → pseudogout  
  - Send fluid for: Gram stain + culture (+ extended culture if anaerobes suspected), crystal analysis (polarised light), cell count + differential, glucose/protein (optional)
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP, ESR  
  - Blood cultures (2 sets)  
  - Urate (note: can be normal in acute gout flare)  
  - Renal function, clotting screen, INR (if on warfarin)
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: X-ray of joint (fracture, osteomyelitis, chondrocalcinosis, OA)  
  - Second-line: Ultrasound (confirms effusion, guides aspiration — essential for hip)  
  - MRI: if suspect osteomyelitis or deep infection not accessible by aspiration (e.g., hip)
- **Special tests:**  
  - Ultrasound guidance for hip aspiration (especially in children; do not attempt blind)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - Kocher criteria for septic arthritis in children (fever, non-weightbearing, ESR>40, WBC>12) — not absolute; clinical suspicion overrides  
  - In adults: treat as septic until proven otherwise if fever + monoarthritis + high CRP
- **Never-miss test:** **Synovial fluid culture + Gram stain** before antibiotics — omission delays diagnosis and destroys joint.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Assess for sepsis: NEWS2, lactate, blood cultures  
  - If septic arthritis suspected: **start empiric IV antibiotics after cultures, but do not delay >1 hour** (even if aspiration fails)  
  - Aspirate joint aseptically (see Drugs & Doses for technique)  
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g PO/IV; avoid NSAIDs until infection excluded
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue IV antibiotics (narrow once sensitivity back)  
  - Daily joint assessment (pain, range, redness, temperature)  
  - Orthopaedic referral for **surgical washout** if:  
    - Hip joint (difficult to drain)  
    - Prosthetic joint  
    - No improvement after 48h medical management  
    - Hypotension/shock or compartment syndrome
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Septic arthritis: IV antibiotics ≥2 weeks then oral switch; total ≥6 weeks (native), longer for prosthetic  
  - Crystal arthropathy: NSAIDs or colchicine for acute flare; urate-lowering therapy (allopurinol) only after flare settled
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline if septic (rate per sepsis protocol)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if immobilised/high risk (but withhold if haemarthrosis)  
  - Diet: normal  
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (more if septic)

### Drugs & Doses
- **Empiric IV antibiotics for septic arthritis (NICE):**  
  - Flucloxacillin 2g IV QDS (native joint)  
  - If penicillin allergy: clindamycin 600mg IV QDS or teicoplanin [check local policy]  
  - If MRSA suspected: add vancomycin (loading dose 15–20 mg/kg IV, then monitor trough)  
  - Prosthetic joint: discuss with microbiology — often flucloxacillin + rifampicin or alternative
- **Local anaesthetic for aspiration:**  
  - Lidocaine 1% (10 mg/mL) without adrenaline  
  - Max dose: 3 mg/kg (e.g., ~21 mL for 70 kg) — **do not exceed**  
  - Typical volume for knee: 5–10 mL. Use 25G needle for skin wheal, then 21G for deep.
- **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g PO/IV QDS. If infection excluded, NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen 500mg PO BD) — contraindicated in AKI, asthma, GI bleeding.
- **Colchicine for acute gout:** 0.5mg PO TDS for 2–3 days. **Reduce in renal impairment** (eGFR <30 → not recommended)
- **Key interactions:**  
  - NSAIDs + anticoagulants → ↑ bleeding risk  
  - Colchicine + macrolides (e.g., clarithromycin) → fatal toxicity — **check all current drugs**
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Vancomycin: dose based on renal function and trough levels  
  - Colchicine: avoid if eGFR <10
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Vancomycin — failure to give loading dose AND check trough after 24h** → toxicity (nephrotoxicity/ototoxicity)  
  - **Colchicine — overdose (max 6mg total course).** Fatal if >0.5mg TDS in elderly / renal impairment. Do not co-prescribe with P-gp or CYP3A4 inhibitors.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Iatrogenic septic arthritis (sterile technique crucial)  
  - Haemarthrosis (especially if INR >3 or platelets <50)  
  - Cartilage damage from needle, nerve/vascular injury (rare: popliteal artery, tibial nerve)  
  - Vasovagal syncope  
  - Cellulitis at puncture site
- **Chronic:**  
  - Joint destruction (septic arthritis — cartilage loss within 8–24h)  
  - Chronic pain, stiffness, secondary OA  
  - Relapse of infection (especially prosthetic joints)  
  - Antibiotic complications: C
